fast.summary: This paper compares two LLM-based approaches for generating structured financial reports from earnings releases: AgenticIR (collaborative agents using the full template) and DecomposedIR (prompt chaining that breaks the template into per-section queries). DecomposedIR statistically significantly outperforms AgenticIR in coverage and detail across both financial and weather-domain datasets.
fast.key_idea: Breaking a multi-section report template into individual section queries (prompt chaining) yields more thorough and detailed outputs than delegating the entire template to collaborative agents.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners building LLM pipelines for structured document generation — financial reports, compliance summaries, standardized briefs — should prefer decomposed prompt chaining over agentic orchestration when coverage and detail matter more than conciseness.
possible_use_cases: Automated generation of structured earnings call summaries for financial analysts
Template-driven compliance report generation from raw regulatory filings
Standardized research report creation from unstructured domain documents

deep.method_breakdown: The paper compares two LLM-based approaches for generating structured financial reports from earnings releases. AgenticIR uses multiple collaborative agents each prompted with the full template to orchestrate report generation. DecomposedIR applies prompt chaining to decompose the template section-by-section, reframing each section as an independent query answered by the LLM using the source document.
deep.key_innovation: The key contribution is an empirical comparison of agentic vs. decomposed IR paradigms for template-adherent document generation, specifically challenging the assumption that agentic frameworks automatically outperform simpler decomposed pipelines. The finding that DecomposedIR statistically significantly outperforms AgenticIR in coverage and detail contradicts the industry trend toward complex multi-agent systems.
deep.technical_insight: The critical design insight is that decomposing a multi-section template into isolated per-section queries reduces the cognitive load on each LLM call, allowing more focused and detailed retrieval per section. AgenticIR suffers from the full-template prompt diluting attention across sections, leading to concise but shallow outputs — a classic case where task decomposition beats end-to-end orchestration.
deep.limitations: The study is limited to financial and weather domains, which may not generalize to other structured report types. Evaluation without human references for the financial dataset relies on automated metrics, which may not fully capture financial accuracy or factual correctness. The paper does not deeply explore failure modes of AgenticIR (e.g., hallucination rates, inter-agent inconsistency) nor does it test hybrid approaches.
interview_questions: Why does DecomposedIR outperform AgenticIR in coverage despite AgenticIR having access to the full template context?
How do you evaluate report quality when no ground-truth human-written references exist for financial documents?
What are the risks of using prompt chaining in DecomposedIR — specifically around cross-section coherence and consistency?
In what scenarios would AgenticIR be preferred over DecomposedIR despite lower coverage scores?
How does this work inform the broader debate about when to use agentic frameworks versus simpler pipeline decomposition?
interview_answers: AgenticIR prompts agents with the full multi-section template, causing attention diffusion — agents optimize for overall coherence and conciseness rather than depth per section. DecomposedIR isolates each section as an independent retrieval query, allowing the LLM to focus entirely on one section's information need at a time, yielding broader and more detailed coverage per section.

The paper uses automated metrics (likely ROUGE, BERTScore, or domain-specific coverage metrics) against the source earnings release to measure how well the generated report covers the key facts. For the weather domain, expert-written reports serve as references. The dual-domain evaluation is a methodological strength to validate findings across reference and reference-free settings.

Prompt chaining in DecomposedIR processes sections independently, so there is no mechanism ensuring that section outputs are consistent with each other or avoid redundancy. A section generated later has no awareness of what was written earlier, potentially leading to contradictions, repeated content, or stylistic inconsistency across the final report.

AgenticIR would be preferred when conciseness and overall narrative coherence matter more than exhaustive coverage — for example, executive summaries or investor briefs where brevity is valued. It may also scale better for very long templates where the decomposed approach incurs prohibitive API call overhead.

The results suggest that agentic complexity does not automatically translate to better output quality — task decomposition with focused prompts often outperforms orchestrated multi-agent systems for structured generation tasks. The key principle is matching the granularity of LLM invocation to the granularity of the output structure, rather than assuming agents will self-organize to handle complexity.
